title: Quiz: Quantum Chaos
description: Test your understanding of quantum chaos, OTOCs, and information scrambling

questions.question: What level spacing distribution P(s) is characteristic of quantum chaotic systems?
questions.explanation: Chaotic quantum systems exhibit Wigner-Dyson level statistics with level repulsion (P(s) vanishes as s approaches 0). The Poisson distribution characterizes integrable systems where energy levels are uncorrelated. The exponent beta depends on the symmetry class (GOE, GUE, GSE).

questions.question: What does an Out-of-Time-Ordered Correlator (OTOC) measure?

options.text: The energy spectrum of a quantum system
The growth of operator non-commutativity and information scrambling
The temperature of a thermal state

questions.explanation: OTOCs measure how operators fail to commute as they evolve in time: C(t) = -<[W(t), V]^2>/2. For chaotic systems, this grows exponentially at early times, signaling the spreading of quantum information and the quantum analog of the butterfly effect.

questions.question: The Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis (ETH) states that:

options.text: All eigenstates have the same energy
Individual energy eigenstates of chaotic systems encode thermal properties
Only the ground state determines thermodynamic properties

questions.explanation: ETH states that in chaotic systems, individual energy eigenstates already encode thermal expectation values: <E_n|O|E_n> approximately equals the microcanonical average at energy E_n. This explains how isolated quantum systems can appear thermal after long times.

questions.question: What is the chaos bound on the Lyapunov exponent for a thermal system at temperature T?

options.text: There is no bound on lambda_L

questions.explanation: The Maldacena-Shenker-Stanford chaos bound states that lambda_L <= 2*pi*k_B*T/hbar. This is a fundamental quantum limit on the rate of information scrambling. Black holes are believed to saturate this bound, making them maximally chaotic systems.

questions.question: A fast scrambler is a system that scrambles information in time proportional to the logarithm of the system size: t_* ~ log(N).
questions.explanation: True. Fast scramblers achieve the minimum possible scrambling time of t_* ~ (1/lambda_L) * log(N), where N is the system size. Black holes are conjectured to be the fastest scramblers in nature. Many-body chaotic systems and certain quantum circuits can approach this limit.

questions.question: The r-ratio statistic <r> for level spacings has approximately what value for GOE (Gaussian Orthogonal Ensemble)?
questions.explanation: The r-ratio statistic <r> ~ 0.386 for Poisson (integrable), ~0.536 for GOE (time-reversal symmetric chaotic), and ~0.603 for GUE (broken time-reversal). These are universal values that don't depend on system details, only on the symmetry class.

questions.question: What is Krylov complexity and how does it behave in chaotic systems?

options.text: Spread of operator in Krylov basis; grows linearly then saturates

questions.explanation: Krylov complexity measures how an operator spreads in the Krylov basis generated by nested commutators with the Hamiltonian. In chaotic systems, it grows linearly with time (after an initial transient) before eventually saturating at a value exponential in system size.

questions.question: Which of the following are signatures of quantum chaos? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Eigenstate thermalization (diagonal matrix elements matching thermal)

questions.explanation: Signatures of quantum chaos include: (a) level repulsion (Wigner-Dyson statistics), (b) exponential OTOC growth indicating scrambling, and (c) ETH with eigenstates encoding thermal properties. Poisson statistics (d) indicate integrable, NOT chaotic systems.
